questions.question_text: In Colorado, if a broker and client have no written agreement specifying the relationship, the default is:
questions.explanation: Colorado's default brokerage relationship is transaction-brokerage. Unless the broker and client agree in writing to a different relationship, the broker acts as a transaction-broker.

questions.question_text: A transaction-broker in Colorado owes which duty to both parties?
questions.explanation: A transaction-broker must exercise reasonable skill and care, present offers, account for money, and disclose material facts — but does not owe loyalty or confidentiality regarding negotiating positions.

questions.question_text: A buyer tells a transaction-broker their maximum purchase price. The broker:
questions.correct_answer: Is not required to keep this confidential
distractors: Must disclose it to the seller immediately
Can share it only with the employing broker
questions.explanation: A transaction-broker does not owe confidentiality regarding negotiating positions. Unlike a single agent, they are not obligated to protect either party's bargaining strategy.

questions.question_text: A transaction-broker learns of a material defect affecting a property. The broker must:
distractors: Disclose it only to the buyer
Keep it confidential for the seller
Report it to the Commission only
questions.explanation: Transaction-brokers must disclose known material facts to both parties. This duty exists regardless of which party may be affected by the information.

questions.question_text: Which fiduciary duty does a transaction-broker in Colorado NOT owe?
questions.explanation: Transaction-brokers must provide skill and care, account for money, and disclose material facts, but they do not owe loyalty or advocacy to either party — they assist both neutrally.

questions.question_text: A single agent in Colorado owes full fiduciary duties. These include all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Single agents owe obedience, loyalty, disclosure, confidentiality, accountability, and reasonable care to their client — not assistance to the opposing party.

questions.question_text: To establish a single-agent relationship in Colorado, the broker must:
distractors: Simply begin working with the client
File a form with the Commission
Have oral consent from the client
questions.explanation: Single agency must be established by written agreement. Since the default is transaction-brokerage, a written agreement is required to change to single agency.

questions.question_text: A seller's single agent learns the buyer is in financial distress. The agent must:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose this to the seller client
questions.explanation: A single agent owes a duty of disclosure to their client. Information that could affect the client's interests must be shared, unlike a transaction-broker who has no advocacy obligation.

questions.question_text: How does the duty of confidentiality differ between a transaction-broker and a single agent?
distractors: There is no difference between them
questions.explanation: Single agents owe confidentiality regarding their client's negotiating position. Transaction-brokers do not owe this duty — they have no obligation to protect either party's bargaining strategy.

questions.question_text: A buyer hires a broker as a single agent. The broker receives an instruction to submit a lowball offer. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Follow the instruction per the duty of obedience
distractors: Submit it only with a higher backup offer
Disclose the buyer's true budget to the seller
questions.explanation: Single agents owe obedience — they must follow lawful instructions from their client. Submitting the offer as directed is required, even if the broker disagrees with the strategy.

questions.question_text: Colorado does not recognize traditional dual agency. Instead, it uses:
questions.explanation: When one brokerage has both the listing and a buyer client, Colorado uses designated brokerage — the employing broker assigns separate brokers to represent each party as single agents.

questions.question_text: In a designated brokerage arrangement, the employing broker's role is:
questions.explanation: The employing broker remains a transaction-broker for the overall transaction while the designated brokers each serve as single agents for their respective clients.

questions.question_text: A brokerage lists a home and also has a buyer client interested in the same property. The employing broker designates separate brokers. Each designated broker owes:
questions.explanation: Each designated broker owes full single-agent fiduciary duties to their respective client, including loyalty, confidentiality, disclosure, and obedience.

questions.question_text: In designated brokerage, confidential information from one client:
distractors: Must be shared with the employing broker
Is disclosed to both parties equally
questions.explanation: Confidential information is kept within each designated broker's relationship. The designated broker for the seller does not share client information with the designated broker for the buyer.

questions.question_text: A small brokerage has only one licensed broker besides the employing broker. Can designated brokerage be used?
questions.correct_answer: No — two separate brokers are needed
distractors: Yes, the employing broker can represent one
Yes, one broker can serve both parties
questions.explanation: Designated brokerage requires two separate brokers to be designated — one for each party. The employing broker remains a transaction-broker and cannot be designated.

questions.question_text: When must a Colorado broker provide a brokerage disclosure to a buyer or seller?
distractors: Only at the time of contract signing
After the inspection period has ended
questions.explanation: The brokerage disclosure explaining available relationship types must be provided at the earliest reasonable opportunity — not delayed until contract signing or later.

questions.question_text: A buyer refuses to sign the brokerage relationship disclosure form. The broker should:
questions.correct_answer: Note it was provided and continue
distractors: Refuse to work with the buyer
Have the buyer sign before any showing
Report the buyer to the Commission
questions.explanation: No signed disclosure is required. The broker must be able to demonstrate the disclosure was provided, but the buyer's signature is not mandatory.

questions.question_text: A client wants to change from transaction-brokerage to single agency mid-transaction. This requires:
questions.correct_answer: A written agreement between the parties
distractors: Consent of the other party in the deal
Starting a new transaction from scratch
questions.explanation: The brokerage relationship can be changed at any time with a written agreement between the broker and client. No Commission approval or third-party consent is needed.

questions.question_text: The brokerage disclosure form explains the types of relationships available. Which relationships must be described?
distractors: Single agent and traditional dual agency
Buyer agency and seller agency only
questions.explanation: The disclosure must explain all available relationship types in Colorado: transaction-broker (default), single agent, and designated brokerage.

questions.question_text: A broker fails to provide the brokerage disclosure to a seller before listing the property. This is:
distractors: Only a problem if the seller complains
questions.explanation: The disclosure must be provided at the earliest reasonable opportunity. Failing to provide it before listing is a violation, regardless of the seller's experience level.
